data.title: Fed Names Horowitz to Lead Office of Inspector General
data.description:
By Matt Grossman

Michael Horowitz, a former internal watchdog at the Justice Department, will lead the Federal Reserve's inspector-general office, the central bank said Friday.

Horowitz succeeds Mark Bialek, who retired from the role in April. For 13 years, Horowitz served as inspector general at the Justice Department. While there he led a high-profile review that found fault with portions of the Federal Bureau of Investigation's examination of alleged links between Donald Trump's 2016 presidential campaign and the Russian government.

Horowitz also chaired a committee of federal inspectors general who oversaw $5 trillion in pandemic-relief spending.

At the Fed, the inspector general's office is responsible for oversight in the form of audits, internal investigations and other efforts to detect waste, fraud and abuse. The head of the Fed's inspector general's office also leads the same function at the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau.

Some Fed critics have advocated for reforming the central bank's inspector-general office. At cabinet-level departments in the federal government, inspectors general are nominated by the president and must be confirmed by the Senate. At the Fed, like at some non-cabinet-level federal agencies, the inspector general is appointed by the body itself.

In May, Senators Elizabeth Warren (D., Mass.) and Rick Scott (R., Fla.) introduced legislation to require presidential appointment of the Fed's inspector general, arguing that an overseer appointed by the central bank isn't empowered to hold leadership accountable.

"This creates an unavoidable conflict of interest," Warren and Scott wrote in a letter to Fed Chair Jerome Powell last month.

Just after his inauguration in January, President Trump dismissed more than a dozen inspectors general across the federal government, a move that critics argued violated rules that govern such firings. Horowitz's job at the Justice Department was spared.

Horowitz has said that a highly politicized environment makes careful work by inspectors general all the more important.

"I think we are still, along with the judiciary, one of the only places that people will turn to and recognize fair, independent, fact-based work," he said at an event at the National Press Foundation earlier this year.

Write to Matt Grossman at matt.grossman@wsj.com

data.title: Medtronic's Separation of Diabetes Business May Drive Long-Term Value, Needham Says
data.description:
Medtronic's planned separation of its diabetes business may create shareholder value in the long term, Needham said Friday in a note.

The separation probably won't "immediately unlock significant value," though "we think that the two companies are likely to see better results as separate entities" in the longer term, Needham said.

Medtronic's margins will increase with the separation and Symplicity, Affera and Hugo will have a bigger impact on the company's growth rate, the report said.

The diabetes segment will be able to decide whether to reinvest free cash flow back into its own business "and should become more nimble and responsive to customer needs and market trends," the report said.

The proposed split is expected to be completed in 18 months, the report said.

Needham has a hold rating on Medtronic stock.

data.title: Chicago grains firm on US weather, US-China trade optimism
data.description:
Key points:
  • Trump, Xi agree to more talks as trade disputes brew
  • Wheat market underpinned by Russia-Ukraine tensions
  • US Weather conditions supported grains

Chicago grains and soybeans firmed on Friday as expectations improved for U.S.-China trade relations after leaders of the two countries spoke and agreed to further discussions.

Additional support came from U.S. weather and escalated fighting between Russia and Ukraine.

The most-active soybean contract on the Chicago Board of Trade (CBOT) settled up 5-1/2 cents at $10.57-1/4 a bushel. Wheat ended up 9-1/4 cents at $5.54-3/4 a bushel, having hit its highest point since May 21, and corn added 3 cents to finish at $4.42-1/2 a bushel.

U.S. President Donald Trump and Chinese leader Xi Jinping confronted weeks of brewing trade tensions in a rare leader-to-leader call on Thursday. Trump said on social media the talks, which focused primarily on trade, led to "a very positive conclusion," announcing further lower-level bilateral talks.

China is by far the world's biggest soybean buyer.

Weather forecasts also added support, said Karl Setzer, partner at Consus Ag Consulting.

"We are finally seeing some risk premium added in," said Setzer.

Rains helped improve soil moisture in parts of the U.S. Southern Plains and the Midwest, according to weather forecaster Vaisala. But dryness lingers in north central Kansas, southeastern Nebraska, Iowa, southern Minnesota, northern Illinois, northern Indiana and western Ohio, according to the forecaster. That dryness could stress germination and early growth of corn and soybeans.

Meanwhile in wheat, temperatures have been below normal in the Plains this week, slowing winter wheat maturation, Vaisala said.

And, in the war between leading wheat suppliers Russia and Ukraine, fighting intensified, with Russia launching an intense missile and drone barrage at the Ukrainian capital Kyiv in the early hours of Friday.

data.title: MOBIVITY HOLDINGS CORP. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Mobivity Holdings Corp., a leading provider of mobile marketing technology, has released its Form 10-Q report for the third quarter of 2025. The report highlights the company's financial performance and operational achievements, showcasing significant growth in revenue and strategic advancements in its business model.

Financial Highlights

Revenues: Mobivity Holdings Corp. reported revenues of $0.51 million for the third quarter of 2025, marking an increase of $0.21 million compared to the same period in 2024. This growth was primarily driven by an increase in Connected Rewards revenue.

Gross Profit: The company achieved a gross profit of $0.27 million, reflecting an improvement from the previous year, attributed to higher revenues.

Loss from Operations: The loss from operations was $(2.06) million, up from $(1.72) million in the same period in 2024, due to higher operating expenses.

Net Loss: Mobivity Holdings Corp. reported a net loss of $(2.59) million, compared to $(2.25) million in the same period in 2024. The increased net loss was impacted by higher interest expenses and settlement losses.

Basic and Diluted Net Loss Per Share: The basic and diluted net loss per share was $(0.04), compared to $(0.03) in the same period in 2024, reflecting the increased net loss.

Business Highlights

Revenue Segments: The Recurrency platform generates revenue through two primary channels: a Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) platform used by convenience and quick service restaurant brands, and the Connected Rewards business, which powers incentivized programs in digital environments.

Geographical Performance: The company derives its revenue primarily from North America, leveraging its Recurrency platform to engage customers through various channels.

Sales Units: Mobivity Holdings Corp. charges customers for each unique action where rewards are delivered, including per install or per individual engagement fees, indicating a focus on performance-based metrics.

Company Strategy: The company aims to build a leading mobile marketing technology product that bridges in-person and digital environments. Its strategy focuses on exploiting competitive advantages, evolving sales infrastructure, acquiring complementary businesses, and building an intellectual property portfolio.

Future Outlook: Mobivity Holdings Corp. plans to continue ramping up the growth of Connected Rewards and is seeking additional capital to fund operations. This indicates a focus on expanding its market presence and operational capabilities.

data.title: Sector Update: Energy
data.description:
Energy stocks gained late Friday afternoon with the NYSE Energy Sector Index rising 1.6% and the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLE) climbing 2%.

The Philadelphia Oil Service Sector index advanced 2%, and the Dow Jones US Utilities index was up 0.4%.

West Texas Intermediate crude oil rose 2.2% to $64.73 a barrel, and global benchmark Brent advanced 1.9% to $66.58 a barrel. Henry Hub natural gas futures gained 2.9% to $3.78 per 1 million BTU.

In corporate news, Sunnova Energy shares popped 5.5%. The company said in a Thursday regulatory filing that it plans to reduce its workforce by about 55%, or 718 employees, in a move to cut operating expenses.
